findings.title: Unencrypted EBS Volume Attached to EC2
findings.description: If an EC2 instance uses an unencrypted EBS volume, anyone who gets access to the disk or a copied snapshot can read the data directly. That is dangerous for databases, logs, and files stored on the instance.
findings.risk: A disk attached to this server is not encrypted. Anyone who obtains the volume or one of its snapshots can read the data directly: databases, logs, config files, credentials. Encryption at rest is the basic control that makes a leaked or copied disk useless to an attacker.
findings.remediation: Create an encrypted copy of the volume (snapshot, encrypted copy, swap) and enable 'EBS encryption by default' so new volumes start encrypted.

findings.title: Security Group Open to Internet on Sensitive Ports
findings.description: A security group open to the whole internet on sensitive ports exposes the service to brute-force attacks, scanning, and exploitation. If the service is compromised, the attacker may gain a foothold in the environment.
findings.evidence: GroupId=sg-0de70b203007c084a, GroupName=capstone-bad-ec2, VpcId=vpc-05a5507b12001c2a9, FromPort=22, ToPort=22, Source=0.0.0.0/0 or ::/0
findings.risk: A security group allows 0.0.0.0/0 or ::/0 on a sensitive port (or on all ports). Every open port is a chance that something vulnerable is listening behind it. Wide-open rules are usually written in a hurry and forgotten, and they invite brute-force attacks and give attackers a foothold if any exposed service is compromised.
findings.remediation: Replace the rule with specific source IPs or ranges, and open only the ports the application actually needs.

findings.title: Security Group Open to Internet on Sensitive Ports
findings.description: A security group open to the whole internet on sensitive ports exposes the service to brute-force attacks, scanning, and exploitation. If the service is compromised, the attacker may gain a foothold in the environment.
findings.evidence: GroupId=sg-0ab0ff10158d197cf, GroupName=capstone-test-open-sg, VpcId=vpc-05a5507b12001c2a9, FromPort=22, ToPort=22, Source=0.0.0.0/0 or ::/0
findings.risk: A security group allows 0.0.0.0/0 or ::/0 on a sensitive port (or on all ports). Every open port is a chance that something vulnerable is listening behind it. Wide-open rules are usually written in a hurry and forgotten, and they invite brute-force attacks and give attackers a foothold if any exposed service is compromised.
findings.remediation: Replace the rule with specific source IPs or ranges, and open only the ports the application actually needs.

findings.title: S3 Bucket Versioning Disabled
findings.description: S3 bucket versioning is not enabled. Accidental deletion or overwrite of objects cannot be undone, and ransomware that overwrites objects may cause permanent data loss.
findings.risk: Without versioning, a single accidental deletion or overwrite permanently destroys the data. Ransomware that overwrites objects has the same effect. Versioning is the primary defence against data-loss events in S3.
findings.remediation: Enable versioning on the bucket. Pair it with lifecycle rules to manage older versions and control storage costs.

findings.title: S3 Access Logging Disabled
findings.description: S3 server access logging is not enabled. Requests to the bucket are not recorded, making it harder to investigate unauthorized access or anomalous activity after the fact.
findings.risk: Without access logs there is no record of who accessed or modified objects. After a breach or data leak, logs are the primary source of evidence. Without them, investigation is severely limited.
findings.remediation: Enable S3 server access logging and direct logs to a separate, protected bucket with appropriate lifecycle rules.

findings.remediation: Enable S3 server access logging and direct logs to a separate, protected bucket with appropriate lifecycle rules.

findings.title: No CloudTrail Trail Configured
findings.description: No CloudTrail trail is configured.
findings.risk: The account has no CloudTrail trail recording API activity. Without it there is no audit trail: a deleted resource, a suspicious login, or misuse of credentials cannot be investigated. Logging is the foundation that every other security control depends on.
findings.remediation: Create a multi-region CloudTrail trail that delivers logs to a private, encrypted S3 bucket.

findings.title: Console User Without MFA
findings.description: 2 IAM user(s) have no MFA configured. If a password is guessed or stolen, those accounts can be logged into with only one factor, which makes account takeover much easier.
findings.risk: These users can sign in to the AWS console with only a password. Passwords get phished, reused, and leaked; without MFA, any leak is a full account takeover. With MFA, the same leak is harmless. For admin and developer accounts, this is one of the cheapest, highest-impact fixes available.
findings.remediation: Require an MFA device for every human IAM user, starting with administrators. Keep a strong password policy, but treat MFA as mandatory on top of it.

findings.title: Root Account Usage or Weak Protection
findings.description: The root account has unrestricted power over the entire AWS account and cannot be constrained by IAM policies. Using or leaving it unprotected is extremely dangerous. Compromise of the root account can lead to total account takeover.
findings.evidence: Root account issues: Root account password was last used: 2026-06-22T14:09:05Z
findings.risk: The root account has unlimited power and cannot be restricted by IAM policies. Signs of day-to-day root usage, missing MFA, or active root access keys mean a single compromise hands over the entire account: data, billing, everything. Root should be locked away with MFA and used only for the rare tasks that genuinely require it.
findings.remediation: Enable MFA on the root account, delete any root access keys, store the credentials safely, and do all regular work through IAM users or roles.

findings.title: Publicly Accessible RDS Database
findings.description: A publicly accessible database can be reached directly from the internet. Attackers can scan the port, brute-force credentials, or try database engine exploits without first compromising another host.
findings.risk: This database is set to publicly accessible. The database is usually the single most valuable target in a system, and public exposure lets attackers scan the port, brute-force credentials, and throw engine exploits at it directly — no other compromise required first.
findings.remediation: Set PubliclyAccessible to false and place the database in a private subnet. Allow access only from application servers, a bastion host, or a VPN, never from 0.0.0.0/0.
findings.db_engine_note: Encryption and access controls are free, built-in engine features on MySQL — there is no licensing barrier to remediation.

findings.title: Weak RDS Backup Retention
findings.description: If backups are kept for too few days, there may be no clean recovery point after ransomware, deletion, or corruption. Recovery becomes much harder when the window is too short.
findings.risk: Automatic backups are disabled or kept for only a few days. When ransomware, an accidental delete, or a slow data-corruption bug hits, the backup window is the difference between a quick restore and permanent data loss — and a short window can close before the problem is even noticed.
findings.remediation: Set the backup retention period to at least 7 to 14 days (or per internal policy), with longer retention for important production databases.
findings.db_engine_note: Encryption and access controls are free, built-in engine features on MySQL — there is no licensing barrier to remediation.

findings.title: Weak RDS Backup Retention
findings.description: If backups are kept for too few days, there may be no clean recovery point after ransomware, deletion, or corruption. Recovery becomes much harder when the window is too short.
findings.risk: Automatic backups are disabled or kept for only a few days. When ransomware, an accidental delete, or a slow data-corruption bug hits, the backup window is the difference between a quick restore and permanent data loss — and a short window can close before the problem is even noticed.
findings.remediation: Set the backup retention period to at least 7 to 14 days (or per internal policy), with longer retention for important production databases.
findings.db_engine_note: Encryption and access controls are free, built-in engine features on MySQL — there is no licensing barrier to remediation.

findings.title: Unencrypted EBS Volume
findings.description: If an EBS volume is not encrypted, anyone who obtains the volume or a snapshot can read the data directly. That is dangerous for backups, databases, and files that contain sensitive information.
findings.risk: This storage volume is not encrypted. If the volume or any snapshot of it is copied to another account or leaks through a backup, the raw data is readable by whoever holds it. Encryption makes that copy worthless to an attacker and is now a standard expectation for serious workloads.
findings.remediation: Encrypt important volumes via snapshot-copy-restore, and enable account-level 'EBS encryption by default' so new volumes are created encrypted automatically.

findings.title: EBS Encryption by Default Disabled
findings.description: If encryption by default is turned off, new volumes can be created unencrypted by mistake. That means the security of future data depends on every user remembering to enable encryption manually.
findings.risk: The account-level setting that automatically encrypts new volumes is turned off. This does not expose existing data, but it guarantees future mistakes: one forgotten checkbox creates the next unencrypted-volume finding. Secure defaults remove the human error from the equation.
findings.remediation: Enable 'EBS encryption by default' in the EC2 account settings so every new volume starts encrypted without extra steps.

findings.title: Security Group: Unrestricted Outbound Traffic
findings.description: This security group allows all outbound traffic to the internet. If a workload is compromised, unrestricted egress makes it easy to exfiltrate data or establish command-and-control communications.
findings.risk: An all-traffic outbound rule (protocol -1) to 0.0.0.0/0 means a compromised workload can exfiltrate data or phone home to a command-and-control server without restriction.
findings.remediation: Restrict outbound rules to known required destinations and ports. Use VPC endpoints for AWS services to eliminate unnecessary internet egress.

findings.title: Unused Security Group
findings.description: This security group is not attached to any network interface. Unused security groups can be accidentally reused with overly permissive rules that were never reviewed for the new workload.
findings.risk: Security groups not attached to any resource accumulate over time. They can contain overly permissive rules that were forgotten, and reuse without review introduces unintended exposure.
findings.remediation: Review and remove unused security groups. Apply consistent naming and tagging to help track ownership and intended use.

findings.title: Security Group: SSH Open to Internet
findings.description: Inbound SSH (port 22) is open to the entire internet. Automated bots continuously scan for open SSH ports and attempt brute-force logins. A single weak credential gives an attacker full shell access to the instance.
findings.risk: Port 22 is reachable from 0.0.0.0/0. Automated bots continuously scan the internet for open SSH ports and attempt credential-stuffing attacks. One weak or reused password gives an attacker full shell access.
findings.remediation: Restrict the SSH rule to specific trusted IP ranges. For production systems, remove public SSH entirely and use AWS Systems Manager Session Manager or a VPN instead.

findings.title: VPC Flow Logs Disabled
findings.description: VPC Flow Logs are not enabled. Network traffic accepted or rejected by this VPC is not recorded, leaving no evidence for incident response, forensic investigation, or anomaly detection.
findings.risk: Without Flow Logs, there is no record of accepted or rejected connections inside the VPC. Incident response and forensic investigations are severely limited — suspicious lateral movement or data exfiltration may go completely undetected.
findings.remediation: Enable VPC Flow Logs for every VPC and deliver records to CloudWatch Logs or S3. Monitor for rejected connections and unexpected traffic patterns.

findings.title: VPC Flow Logs Disabled
findings.description: VPC Flow Logs are not enabled. Network traffic accepted or rejected by this VPC is not recorded, leaving no evidence for incident response, forensic investigation, or anomaly detection.
findings.risk: Without Flow Logs, there is no record of accepted or rejected connections inside the VPC. Incident response and forensic investigations are severely limited — suspicious lateral movement or data exfiltration may go completely undetected.
findings.remediation: Enable VPC Flow Logs for every VPC and deliver records to CloudWatch Logs or S3. Monitor for rejected connections and unexpected traffic patterns.

findings.title: Route Table Has Default Route to Internet Gateway
findings.description: A route table has a default route (0.0.0.0/0) pointing to an Internet Gateway. Subnets associated with this route table have direct internet connectivity, and resources placed there may become publicly reachable if security groups allow it.
findings.risk: A default route (0.0.0.0/0) pointing to an Internet Gateway makes associated subnets public. Resources in these subnets are internet-reachable when they have a public IP and permissive security groups. Sensitive workloads placed here by mistake are directly exposed.
findings.remediation: Confirm only intentionally public subnets use this route table. Move databases and internal services to private subnets. Use a NAT Gateway for outbound-only internet access from private subnets.

findings.title: Route Table Has Default Route to Internet Gateway
findings.description: A route table has a default route (0.0.0.0/0) pointing to an Internet Gateway. Subnets associated with this route table have direct internet connectivity, and resources placed there may become publicly reachable if security groups allow it.
findings.evidence: RouteTableId=rtb-0a2e1b6264bfe2626, VpcId=vpc-02eb2bfbb600597ce, InternetGateway=igw-03add25660d9d1db7, AssociatedSubnets=main route table (no explicit subnet association)
findings.risk: A default route (0.0.0.0/0) pointing to an Internet Gateway makes associated subnets public. Resources in these subnets are internet-reachable when they have a public IP and permissive security groups. Sensitive workloads placed here by mistake are directly exposed.
findings.remediation: Confirm only intentionally public subnets use this route table. Move databases and internal services to private subnets. Use a NAT Gateway for outbound-only internet access from private subnets.

findings.title: Subnet Auto-Assigns Public IPv4 Addresses
findings.description: This subnet automatically assigns a public IPv4 address to every instance launched in it. New resources may be unintentionally exposed to the internet if their security group allows inbound traffic.
findings.evidence: SubnetId=subnet-0875769f9dc42b4cf, VpcId=vpc-02eb2bfbb600597ce, AvailabilityZone=ca-central-1a, MapPublicIpOnLaunch=True, RouteClassification=No explicit public route detected
findings.risk: Auto-assignment of public IPs means new resources become internet-reachable by default. A permissive security group — even a temporary one — immediately exposes the instance. The risk is compounded because it happens silently without any explicit action.
findings.remediation: Disable MapPublicIpOnLaunch on subnets that are not intentionally public. Reserve public subnets for load balancers and bastion hosts only.

findings.title: Subnet Auto-Assigns Public IPv4 Addresses

findings.evidence: SubnetId=subnet-033a956bc7080b6e7, VpcId=vpc-02eb2bfbb600597ce, AvailabilityZone=ca-central-1b, MapPublicIpOnLaunch=True, RouteClassification=No explicit public route detected
findings.risk: Auto-assignment of public IPs means new resources become internet-reachable by default. A permissive security group — even a temporary one — immediately exposes the instance. The risk is compounded because it happens silently without any explicit action.
findings.remediation: Disable MapPublicIpOnLaunch on subnets that are not intentionally public. Reserve public subnets for load balancers and bastion hosts only.

findings.evidence: SubnetId=subnet-03206f986cd602791, VpcId=vpc-02eb2bfbb600597ce, AvailabilityZone=ca-central-1d, MapPublicIpOnLaunch=True, RouteClassification=No explicit public route detected
findings.risk: Auto-assignment of public IPs means new resources become internet-reachable by default. A permissive security group — even a temporary one — immediately exposes the instance. The risk is compounded because it happens silently without any explicit action.
findings.remediation: Disable MapPublicIpOnLaunch on subnets that are not intentionally public. Reserve public subnets for load balancers and bastion hosts only.

findings.title: KMS Key Rotation Disabled
findings.description: Automatic key rotation is disabled for this customer-managed KMS key. Long-lived keys increase the window of exposure if a key is ever compromised. Annual rotation is a standard control for key hygiene.
findings.risk: Automatic key rotation is off. Without rotation, the same key material is used indefinitely. If it is ever extracted or compromised, all data encrypted with it is at risk for the full lifetime of the key. Annual rotation limits that window.
findings.remediation: Enable automatic annual rotation for the key. Verify that dependent services handle rotation transparently before enabling it.
